inlineContent.text: Arrays are one of the most commonly used data types in an app. You use
arrays to organize your app’s data. Specifically, you use the
type
to hold elements of a single type, the array’s
type. An array
can store any kind of elements—from integers to strings to classes.
Swift makes it easy to create arrays in your code using an array literal:
simply surround a comma-separated list of values with square brackets.
Without any other information, Swift creates an array that includes the
specified values, automatically inferring the array’s
type. For
example:
You can create an empty array by specifying the
type of your
array in the declaration. For example:

code: // The full type name is also allowed

inlineContent.text: If you need an array that is preinitialized with a fixed number of default
values, use the
initializer.
When you need to perform an operation on all of an array’s elements, use a
loop to iterate through the array’s contents.
Use the
property to check quickly whether an array has any
elements, or use the
property to find the number of elements in
the array.

code: print("I don't know any odd numbers.")

inlineContent.text: Use the
and
properties for safe access to the value of the
array’s first and last elements. If the array is empty, these properties
are

code: if let firstElement = oddNumbers.first, let lastElement = oddNumbers.last {

inlineContent.text: You can access individual array elements through a subscript. The first
element of a nonempty array is always at index zero. You can subscript an
array with any integer from zero up to, but not including, the count of
the array. Using a negative number or an index equal to or greater than
triggers a runtime error. For example:

code: // Triggers runtime error: Index out of range

inlineContent.text: Suppose you need to store a list of the names of students that are signed
up for a class you’re teaching. During the registration period, you need
to add and remove names as students add and drop the class.
To add single elements to the end of an array, use the
method.
Add multiple elements at the same time by passing another array or a
sequence of any kind to the
method.
You can add new elements in the middle of an array by using the
method for single elements and by using
to insert multiple elements from another
collection or array literal. The elements at that index and later indices
are shifted back to make room.
To remove elements from an array, use the
, and
methods.

code: // Ben's family is moving to another state
// William is signing up for a different class

inlineContent.text: You can replace an existing element with a new value by assigning the new
value to the subscript.

content.text: Growing the Size of an Array

inlineContent.text: Every array reserves a specific amount of memory to hold its contents. When
you add elements to an array and that array begins to exceed its reserved
capacity, the array allocates a larger region of memory and copies its
elements into the new storage. The new storage is a multiple of the old
storage’s size. This exponential growth strategy means that appending an
element happens in constant time, averaging the performance of many append
operations. Append operations that trigger reallocation have a performance
cost, but they occur less and less often as the array grows larger.
If you know approximately how many elements you will need to store, use the
method before appending to the array to avoid
intermediate reallocations. Use the
and
properties to
determine how many more elements the array can store without allocating
larger storage.
For arrays of most
types, this storage is a contiguous block of
memory. For arrays with an
type that is a class or
protocol type, this storage can be a contiguous block of memory or an
instance of
. Because any arbitrary subclass of
can
become an
, there are no guarantees about representation or
efficiency in this case.
Each array has an independent value that includes the values of all of its
elements. For simple types such as integers and other structures, this
means that when you change a value in one array, the value of that element
does not change in any copies of the array. For example:
If the elements in an array are instances of a class, the semantics are the
same, though they might appear different at first. In this case, the
values stored in the array are references to objects that live outside the
array. If you change a reference to an object in one array, only that
array has a reference to the new object. However, if two arrays contain
references to the same object, you can observe changes to that object’s
properties from both arrays. For example:

code: // An integer type with reference semantics
// Modifications to an instance are visible from either array
// Replacements, additions, and removals are still visible

inlineContent.text: Arrays, like all variable-size collections in the standard library, use
copy-on-write optimization. Multiple copies of an array share the same
storage until you modify one of the copies. When that happens, the array
being modified replaces its storage with a uniquely owned copy of itself,
which is then modified in place. Optimizations are sometimes applied that
can reduce the amount of copying.
This means that if an array is sharing storage with other copies, the first
mutating operation on that array incurs the cost of copying the array. An
array that is the sole owner of its storage can perform mutating
operations in place.
In the example below, a
array is created along with two copies
that share the same storage. When the original
array is
modified, it makes a unique copy of its storage before making the
modification. Further modifications to
are made in place, while
the two copies continue to share the original storage.

code: // The storage for 'numbers' is copied here

inlineContent.text: When you need to access APIs that require data in an
instance
instead of
, use the type-cast operator (
) to bridge your
instance. For bridging to be possible, the
type of your array
must be a class, an
protocol (a protocol imported from Objective-C
or marked with the
attribute), or a type that bridges to a
Foundation type.
The following example shows how you can bridge an
instance to
to use the
method. In this example, the
array can be bridged to
because the
array’s
elements bridge to
. The compiler prevents bridging the
array, on the other hand, because its
type is
, which does
not
bridge to a Foundation type.

code: // error: cannot convert value of type '[String?]' to type 'NSArray'

inlineContent.text: Bridging from
to
takes O(1) time and O(1) space if the
array’s elements are already instances of a class or an
protocol;
otherwise, it takes O(
n
) time and space.
When the destination array’s element type is a class or an
protocol, bridging from
to
first calls the
in Objective-C) method on the array to get an immutable
copy and then performs additional Swift bookkeeping work that takes O(1)
time. For instances of
that are already immutable,
usually returns the same array in O(1) time; otherwise, the copying
performance is unspecified. If
returns the same array, the
instances of
and
share storage using the same
copy-on-write optimization that is used when two instances of
share storage.
When the destination array’s element type is a nonclass type that bridges
to a Foundation type, bridging from
to
performs a
bridging copy of the elements to contiguous storage in O(
n
) time. For
example, bridging from
to
performs such a copy. No
further bridging is required when accessing elements of the
instance.
The
and
types are not bridged;
instances of those types always have a contiguous block of memory as
their storage.

abstract.text: An ordered, random-access collection.
In addition to using an array literal, you can also create an array using these initializers.
